fact: A product is a substance that is made in a chemical reaction.
acceptedAnswers: substance made in a chemical reaction
explanation: Products are made during chemical reactions and appear on the right of a word equation.

fact: A reactant is a starting substance in a chemical reaction.
explanation: Reactants are the substances present before the reaction starts.

fact: A word equation represents a chemical reaction simply.
explanation: A word equation shows reactants on the left, products on the right, and an arrow between them.

fact: In a word equation, the arrow means reacts to make.
explanation: The arrow shows that the reactants react to make the products.

fact: Chemical reactions can be compared with physical changes.
acceptedAnswers: physical changes do not make new products
explanation: The source asks pupils to compare chemical reactions to physical changes, focusing on whether new products are made.
commonMistakes: Saying every change is a chemical reaction

fact: An acid is a solution with a pH value less than 7.
explanation: The checklist glossary defines an acid as a solution below pH 7.

fact: An alkali is a soluble base.
explanation: A base that dissolves in water is called an alkali.
commonMistakes: Saying every base is an alkali

fact: A base is a substance that neutralises an acid.
explanation: The mindmap adds that an alkali is a soluble base.
commonMistakes: Thinking a base is always an acid

fact: Hydrochloric acid and sulfuric acid are examples of acids in the revision spread.
explanation: These two examples are named in the acids revision spread.
commonMistakes: Giving sodium hydroxide as an acid

fact: Sodium hydroxide and potassium hydroxide are examples of alkalis in the revision spread.
explanation: These are the named alkali examples in the revision spread.

fact: A corrosive substance can burn your skin or eyes.
explanation: The glossary defines corrosive as able to burn skin or eyes.

fact: An irritant substance makes your skin itch or swell up a little.
acceptedAnswers: makes your skin itch or swell
explanation: Irritant is a hazard term from the glossary.

fact: Concentrated hydrochloric acid is more corrosive than dilute hydrochloric acid.
explanation: This comparison appears directly on the acids revision spread.
commonMistakes: Saying dilute hydrochloric acid is more corrosive

fact: Concentration is a measure of the number of particles in a given volume.
acceptedAnswers: number of particles in a given volume
explanation: This is the glossary definition of concentration.

fact: A dilute solution has a small number of solute particles per unit volume.
acceptedAnswers: small number of solute particles per unit volume
explanation: Dilute describes how many solute particles are in each litre or cubic metre.

fact: An indicator is used to identify whether an unknown solution is acidic or alkaline.
acceptedAnswers: identify whether a solution is acidic or alkaline
explanation: The mindmap and glossary both define indicators this way.

fact: Litmus paper and universal indicator are examples of chemical indicators.
explanation: Both examples appear on the revision spread.
commonMistakes: Giving hydrochloric acid as an indicator

fact: Blue litmus paper goes red when acid is added.
explanation: The glossary states blue litmus paper goes red on adding acid.

fact: Red litmus paper goes blue when alkali is added.
explanation: The glossary states red litmus paper goes blue on adding alkali.

fact: Universal indicator changes colour to show the pH of a solution.
explanation: Universal indicator is a mixture of dyes used to show pH.
commonMistakes: Thinking universal indicator only says acid or alkali

fact: The pH scale runs from 0 to 14.
explanation: The acids revision spread asks this and gives 0-14.

fact: A solution of pH 7 is neutral.
explanation: The checklist glossary and mindmap both identify pH 7 as neutral.

fact: The pH scale shows whether a substance is acidic, alkaline, or neutral.
acceptedAnswers: shows whether acidic alkaline or neutral
explanation: This is the glossary definition of the pH scale.

fact: Stomach acid is pH 2 in the revision spread.
explanation: The revision spread lists stomach acid as pH 2.

fact: Bleach is pH 13 in the revision spread.
explanation: The revision spread lists bleach as pH 13.

fact: Pure water is pH 7 in the revision spread.
explanation: Pure water is listed as neutral, pH 7.

fact: Cola, lemon juice and vinegar are acidic in the revision spread table.
explanation: The source table marks these substances as acids.
commonMistakes: Putting vinegar in the alkali column

fact: Drain cleaner, toothpaste and oven cleaner are alkaline in the revision spread table.
acceptedAnswers: drain cleaner toothpaste and oven cleaner
explanation: The source table marks these substances as alkalis.

fact: Sugar solution and pure water are neutral in the revision spread table.
explanation: The source table marks sugar solution and pure water as neutral.

fact: A strong acid is an acid in which all of the acid particles split up when it dissolves in water.
explanation: This is the checklist glossary definition of strong acid.
commonMistakes: Saying strong acid means concentrated acid

fact: A weak acid is an acid in which only some of the acid particles split up when it dissolves in water.
acceptedAnswers: only some acid particles split up
explanation: This is the checklist glossary definition of weak acid.
commonMistakes: Saying weak acid means dilute acid

fact: Neutralisation is when an acid cancels out a base or a base cancels out an acid.
explanation: The checklist glossary defines neutralisation as acid and base cancelling each other out.
commonMistakes: Saying neutralisation means adding water only

fact: Acid plus alkali makes salt and water.
explanation: The revision spread gives the general neutralisation equation acid + alkali -> salt + water.

fact: Neutralisation has been reached with universal indicator when the solution turns green.
explanation: The mindmap says the solution turns green when neutralisation is reached using universal indicator.
commonMistakes: Stopping when the solution turns red

fact: Salty water is pH 7 in the revision spread.
explanation: The revision spread directly gives salty water as pH 7.
commonMistakes: Saying salty water is strongly acidic

fact: Hydrochloric acid plus sodium hydroxide makes sodium chloride and water.
explanation: This completed equation appears on the acids revision spread.

fact: Sulfuric acid plus copper oxide makes copper sulfate and water.
explanation: This completed equation appears on the acids revision spread.

fact: Lithium hydroxide plus sulfuric acid makes lithium sulfate and water.
explanation: This completed equation appears on the acids revision spread.

fact: A salt is a compound in which the hydrogen atoms of an acid are replaced by atoms of a metal element.
acceptedAnswers: hydrogen atoms replaced by metal atoms
hydrogen atoms of an acid are replaced
explanation: This is the glossary definition of a salt.
commonMistakes: Thinking salt only means table salt

fact: The reaction between an acid and base forms a salt.
explanation: The checklist asks pupils to state products formed when an acid reacts with a base.
commonMistakes: Saying acid plus base makes only acid

fact: The source asks pupils to choose salts formed when acids react with metals or bases.
acceptedAnswers: choose the salt from the acid and metal or base
explanation: Salt names come from the reacting acid and metal/base examples in the source.

fact: The acids source includes harmful, corrosive, explosive, flammable, toxic, and harmful to the environment hazard symbols.
acceptedAnswers: harmful corrosive explosive flammable toxic harmful to the environment
explanation: These six hazard symbols are named on the revision spread.

fact: A neutral solution turns universal indicator green.
explanation: The revision spread says neutralisation is reached when universal indicator turns green.

fact: Weak acid is labelled below strong acid and before neutral on the pH scale in the revision spread.
explanation: Weak acid is still acidic, so it is below pH 7 but closer to neutral than a strong acid.

fact: Weak alkali is labelled after neutral and before strong alkali on the pH scale in the revision spread.
explanation: Weak alkali is above pH 7 but closer to neutral than strong alkali.

fact: A food chain is part of a food web, starting with a producer and ending with a top predator.
acceptedAnswers: starts with a producer and ends with a top predator
explanation: This is the glossary definition of food chain.
commonMistakes: Starting a food chain with a top predator

fact: A food chain diagram shows the transfer of energy between organisms.
explanation: The glossary says food chains show energy transfer.
commonMistakes: Saying arrows show what gets eaten

fact: Food chains show the direction in which energy flows when one organism eats another.
explanation: The ecosystems knowledge organiser gives this wording.

fact: A food web shows how food chains in an ecosystem are linked.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of food web.
commonMistakes: Thinking a food web is one straight chain

fact: A producer is a green plant or algae that makes its own food using sunlight by photosynthesis.
acceptedAnswers: green plant or algae that makes its own food
explanation: This is the source definition of producer.

fact: A consumer is an animal that eats other animals or plants.
acceptedAnswers: animal that eats other animals or plants
explanation: This is the source definition of consumer.
commonMistakes: Saying consumers make their own food

fact: A predator is an animal that eats other animals.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of predator.
commonMistakes: Calling the eaten animal the predator

fact: Prey is an animal that is eaten by another animal.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of prey.

fact: A decomposer breaks down dead plant and animal material so nutrients can be recycled back to soil or water.
acceptedAnswers: breaks down dead plant and animal material
explanation: This is the source definition of decomposer.
commonMistakes: Thinking decomposers only eat live animals

subtopic: Disruption to food chains and webs
fact: Interdependence is the way living organisms depend on each other to survive, grow, and reproduce.
acceptedAnswers: depend on each other to survive grow and reproduce
explanation: This is the glossary definition of interdependence.
commonMistakes: Thinking organisms are independent in a food web

subtopic: Disruption to food chains and webs
fact: Bioaccumulation is the build-up of toxic chemicals inside organisms in a food chain.
explanation: This is the source definition of bioaccumulation.

subtopic: Disruption to food chains and webs
fact: The source asks pupils to explain the importance of insect pollinators to food supplies.
explanation: Pollinators help flowering plants reproduce, which matters for food supplies.

fact: An ecosystem is the living things in a given area and their non-living environment.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of ecosystem.

fact: A community is the collection of different types of organism present in an ecosystem.
acceptedAnswers: different types of organism in an ecosystem
explanation: This is the source definition of community.

fact: A habitat is the area in which an organism lives.
acceptedAnswers: area in which an organism lives
explanation: This is the glossary definition of habitat.

fact: The environment is the surrounding air, water, and soil where an organism lives.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of environment.

fact: A niche is a particular place or role that an organism has in an ecosystem.
explanation: This is the source definition of niche.

fact: A population is a group of the same species living in an area.
acceptedAnswers: same species living in an area
explanation: This is the glossary definition of population.

fact: Competition means competing with other organisms for resources.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of competition.

fact: Animals compete for food, water, space and mates.
explanation: The ecosystems knowledge organiser states these animal resources.

fact: Plants compete for light, water, space and minerals.
explanation: The ecosystems knowledge organiser states these plant resources.

fact: As predator numbers increase, prey numbers decrease because more prey are eaten.
acceptedAnswers: prey decrease because more are eaten
explanation: This predator-prey relationship appears on the knowledge organiser.
commonMistakes: Saying prey always increase when predators increase

fact: As predator numbers decrease, prey numbers increase because fewer prey are eaten.
acceptedAnswers: prey increase because fewer are eaten
explanation: This predator-prey relationship appears on the knowledge organiser.
commonMistakes: Saying prey decrease when fewer predators are present

fact: The stamen is the male reproductive part of the flower.
explanation: The glossary says stamen means the male reproductive parts of the flower.

fact: The anther is the male part of a flower that produces pollen.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of anther.

fact: The filament holds up the anther.
explanation: This is the source definition of filament.

fact: The carpel is the female part of the flower, made up of the stigma, style, and ovary.
acceptedAnswers: female part made of stigma style and ovary
explanation: This is the source definition of carpel.

fact: The stigma is sticky to catch grains of pollen.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of stigma.

fact: The style holds up the stigma.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of style.

fact: The ovary contains ovules.
explanation: This is the source definition of ovary.

fact: Ovules are female sex cells in plants found in the ovary.
acceptedAnswers: female sex cells found in the ovary
explanation: This is the glossary definition of ovule.

fact: Pollen contains the plant male sex cells found on the stamens.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of pollen.

fact: Petals are brightly coloured parts of a flower that attract insects.
explanation: This is the source definition of petal.

fact: Sepals are special leaves under the flower that protect unopened buds.
explanation: This is the source definition of sepal.

fact: Pollination is the transfer of pollen from the male part of the flower to the female part on the same or another plant.
acceptedAnswers: transfer of pollen from male part to female part
explanation: This is the source definition of pollination.

fact: Fertilisation is the joining of a nucleus from a male and female sex cell.
acceptedAnswers: joining of a nucleus from male and female sex cell
explanation: This is the glossary definition of fertilisation.

fact: After fertilisation, the ovary becomes a fruit that contains seeds.
explanation: The glossary says fruit is the structure the ovary becomes after fertilisation and contains seeds.

fact: A seed contains the embryo of a new plant.
acceptedAnswers: contains the embryo of a new plant
explanation: This is the glossary definition of seed.

fact: Germination is the process of a seed starting to grow into a seedling.
acceptedAnswers: seed starting to grow into a seedling
explanation: This is the glossary definition of germination.

fact: For germination, a seed needs water, oxygen and warmth.
explanation: The organiser says water, oxygen and warmth are needed for germination.
commonMistakes: Saying seeds always need light to germinate

fact: Seed dispersal is the movement of seeds away from the parent plant.
acceptedAnswers: movement of seeds away from the parent plant
explanation: This is the glossary definition of seed dispersal.

fact: The source asks pupils to state ways seeds can be dispersed.
explanation: Seed dispersal methods are part of the checklist, and questions should focus on matching seeds to their method.

fact: The source asks pupils to describe how a seed is adapted to its method of dispersal.
acceptedAnswers: adapted to its method of dispersal
explanation: A good answer links the seed feature to how it moves away.
commonMistakes: Naming a feature without linking it to dispersal

fact: Seed dispersal is important to the survival of the parent plant and its offspring.
acceptedAnswers: important to survival of parent and offspring
explanation: The checklist specifically asks pupils to explain why dispersal matters for parent and offspring survival.
commonMistakes: Saying dispersal is only for animals

subtopic: Disruption to food chains and webs
fact: If one organism dies out, organisms that eat it may die out unless they have a different food source.
acceptedAnswers: organisms that eat it may die out unless they have another food source
explanation: This idea is shown in the disruption section of the knowledge organiser.
commonMistakes: Thinking only one organism is affected

fact: A hazard is a situation that presents a threat to people.
acceptedAnswers: situation that presents a threat to people
explanation: This is the glossary definition of hazard.

fact: A risk is how likely something is to be harmful.
acceptedAnswers: how likely something is to be harmful
explanation: This is the glossary definition of risk.
commonMistakes: Saying risk and hazard are identical

fact: A precaution is an action taken to remove a hazard or reduce exposure to it.
acceptedAnswers: action to remove hazard or reduce exposure
explanation: This is the glossary definition of precaution.

fact: A risk assessment describes how to make injury or equipment damage less likely, and what to do if it happens.
acceptedAnswers: make injury or equipment damage less likely
what to do if it happens
explanation: This is the glossary definition of risk assessment.

fact: The source expects pupils to identify and draw commonly used laboratory apparatus.
explanation: Questions should ask for equipment choices from procedures, as the checklist states.

fact: The source expects pupils to describe the function of each piece of equipment.
explanation: Equipment questions should connect apparatus to its job.

fact: The source expects pupils to identify equipment needed from a procedure description.
explanation: This objective is from the Skills checklist.
commonMistakes: Choosing equipment unrelated to the procedure

fact: The source expects pupils to identify the parts of a Bunsen burner.
acceptedAnswers: identify parts of a Bunsen burner
explanation: Bunsen burner parts are a checklist objective.

fact: The source expects pupils to know how to use a Bunsen burner safely.
explanation: Safety questions should link a hazard to a precaution.

fact: The source expects pupils to explain why the three different flames of a Bunsen burner occur.
explanation: This is one of the Bunsen burner objectives from the checklist.
commonMistakes: Only naming a flame without explaining

fact: A variable is a factor that can be changed, measured and controlled.
acceptedAnswers: factor that can be changed measured and controlled
explanation: This is the glossary definition of variable.

fact: The independent variable is what you change in an investigation.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of independent variable.

fact: The dependent variable is what you measure or observe when you change the independent variable.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of dependent variable.

fact: A control variable remains unchanged or is held constant to stop it affecting the dependent variable.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of control variable.

fact: Keeping control variables constant ensures the investigation is valid.
explanation: This explanation is included in the control variable definition.
commonMistakes: Saying controls only make it neat

fact: A fair test finds out how one variable affects another while all other variables are kept constant.
acceptedAnswers: one variable affects another while all other variables are constant
explanation: This is the glossary definition of fair test.
commonMistakes: Changing lots of variables at once

fact: A prediction is a statement that says what you think will happen in an experiment.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of prediction.
commonMistakes: Writing a question instead of a prediction

fact: A hypothesis is an explanation you can test that includes a reason and a science idea.
acceptedAnswers: testable explanation with a reason and science idea
explanation: This is the glossary definition of hypothesis.
commonMistakes: Calling a hypothesis a random guess

fact: An investigation is an experiment or set of experiments designed to produce data to answer a scientific question or test a theory.
acceptedAnswers: answer a scientific question or test a theory
explanation: This is the glossary definition of investigation.
commonMistakes: Thinking investigation only means looking around

fact: A plan describes how equipment will be used to collect valid data to answer a scientific question.
acceptedAnswers: description of how equipment will collect valid data
explanation: This is the glossary definition of plan.
commonMistakes: Writing only a list of equipment

fact: Data are words or numbers obtained when you make observations or measurements.
acceptedAnswers: words or numbers from observations or measurements
explanation: This is the glossary definition of data.
commonMistakes: Thinking data must only be numbers

fact: An observation is information gathered by your senses.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of observation.
commonMistakes: Thinking observation must use a machine

fact: A results table should present data appropriately.
explanation: This objective is from the Skills checklist.
commonMistakes: Putting results in a messy list

fact: The mean is an average calculated by adding all values and dividing by the number of values.
acceptedAnswers: add all values and divide by number of values
explanation: This is the glossary definition of mean.

fact: A bar chart displays values of categories and is used when the independent variable is discontinuous.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of bar chart.
commonMistakes: Using a line graph for categories

fact: A line graph shows the relationship between two continuous variables.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of line graph.
commonMistakes: Using a line graph for eye colour categories

fact: Continuous data has values that can be any number.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of continuous.

fact: Discontinuous data has values that are words or discrete numbers.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of discontinuous.
commonMistakes: Thinking discontinuous means any measured value

fact: A line of best fit is a straight or curved smooth line drawn to show the pattern of data points.
acceptedAnswers: smooth line showing pattern of data points
explanation: This is the source definition of line of best fit.

fact: The line of best fit travels through or very close to as many plotted points as possible.
acceptedAnswers: through or close to as many points as possible
explanation: This wording comes from the glossary.
commonMistakes: Forcing the line through every point

fact: A conclusion says what you have found out during an investigation.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of conclusion.
commonMistakes: Writing a prediction as a conclusion

fact: The source expects pupils to identify patterns in data using a graph.
explanation: This objective is from the Skills checklist.
commonMistakes: Describing a pattern without using the graph

fact: The source expects pupils to interpret data to suggest relationships between variables and draw conclusions.
explanation: This objective is from the Skills checklist.

fact: To evaluate means to discuss the quality of data collected and suggest improvements to the method.
acceptedAnswers: discuss data quality and suggest improvements
explanation: This is the glossary definition of evaluate.

fact: Precise describes a set of repeat measurements that are close together.
acceptedAnswers: repeat measurements that are close together
explanation: This is the glossary definition of precise.
commonMistakes: Confusing precision with getting the right answer

fact: Repeatable means repeat readings are close together.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of repeatable.

fact: An investigation is valid if it is designed appropriately, including keeping control variables constant.
acceptedAnswers: designed appropriately with control variables constant
explanation: This is the glossary definition of valid.
commonMistakes: Saying valid means colourful or neat

fact: The source expects pupils to suggest improvements that increase the quality of data.
acceptedAnswers: suggest improvements to increase data quality
explanation: This objective is from the Skills checklist.

fact: The source expects pupils to plan a simple experiment and write a method.
explanation: A method should explain what to do clearly enough to collect data.

fact: The source expects pupils to consider hazards and safety precautions needed for an experiment.
explanation: Planning practical work includes safety.

fact: The source expects pupils to explain why controlling variables is important.
explanation: Control variables stop other factors affecting the dependent variable.

fact: The source expects pupils to explain why it is important for an experiment to be repeated.
acceptedAnswers: makes results more repeatable or reliable
explanation: Repeats help judge whether readings are close together and trustworthy.

fact: Scientific enquiries include observation over time, fair test, and pattern seeking.
acceptedAnswers: observation over time fair test and pattern seeking
explanation: These enquiry types are named in the glossary.
commonMistakes: Thinking there is only one enquiry type

fact: A good graph uses appropriate scales and axis labels.
explanation: The checklist says pupils should use appropriate scales and axis labels when plotting graphs.

fact: When plotting a graph, pupils should plot points and draw the line or curve of best fit.
acceptedAnswers: plot points and draw line or curve of best fit
line or curve of best fit
explanation: This is directly from the graphing objective.
commonMistakes: Joining bars instead of plotting points

fact: A wave is an oscillation or vibration which transfers energy from one place to another.
acceptedAnswers: oscillation or vibration that transfers energy
explanation: This wording comes from the waves knowledge organiser.

fact: Amplitude is the maximum amount of vibration measured from the middle position of the wave.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of amplitude.

fact: Amplitude is usually measured in metres.
explanation: The glossary states amplitude is usually measured in metres.

fact: Wavelength is the distance between two corresponding points on a wave, in metres.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of wavelength.

fact: Frequency is the number of waves produced in one second, in hertz.
acceptedAnswers: number of waves produced in one second
explanation: This is the glossary definition of frequency.

fact: Hertz is the unit of frequency.
explanation: The glossary defines hertz as the unit of frequency.

fact: A crest or peak is the top of a wave.
explanation: The glossary lists crest and peak as the top of a wave.

fact: A trough is the bottom of a wave.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of trough.

fact: A transverse wave travels at 90 degrees to the direction of energy transfer.
acceptedAnswers: at right angles to energy transfer
explanation: The waves organiser gives light as an example of a transverse wave.
commonMistakes: Saying transverse travels in the same direction as energy

fact: A longitudinal wave vibrates in the same direction as the wave or energy transfer.
explanation: Sound is given as an example of a longitudinal wave.

fact: The speed of sound is 330 m/s.
explanation: The checklist glossary gives speed of sound as 330 m/s.
commonMistakes: Saying sound travels at 300 million m/s

fact: Sound waves are caused by vibration of particles.
explanation: The waves organiser states this directly.
commonMistakes: Saying sound can travel in a vacuum

fact: Sound needs a medium to travel through.
explanation: The organiser says longitudinal waves such as sound need a medium.
commonMistakes: Saying sound travels through a vacuum

fact: A vacuum is a space with no particles of matter in it.
acceptedAnswers: space with no particles of matter
explanation: This is the glossary definition of vacuum.
commonMistakes: Thinking vacuum means full of air

fact: An echo is reflection of sound waves from a surface back to the listener.
acceptedAnswers: reflection of sound waves back to listener
explanation: This is the glossary definition of echo.

fact: Absorption is when energy is transferred from sound or other waves to a material.
acceptedAnswers: energy transferred from waves to a material
explanation: This is the glossary definition of absorption.

fact: To amplify means to increase the amplitude of a sound so that it sounds louder.
acceptedAnswers: increase amplitude so sound is louder
explanation: This is the glossary definition of amplify.

fact: Volume means how loud or quiet a sound is, in decibels.
acceptedAnswers: how loud or quiet a sound is
explanation: This is the glossary definition of volume.
commonMistakes: Using hertz as the unit of volume

fact: A decibel is a commonly used unit of sound intensity or loudness.
acceptedAnswers: unit of sound intensity or loudness
explanation: This is the glossary definition of decibel.

fact: Pitch is how high or low a sound is.
acceptedAnswers: how high or low a sound is
explanation: This is the glossary definition of pitch.

fact: A low pitch sound has a low frequency and a high pitch sound has a high frequency.
acceptedAnswers: low pitch low frequency high pitch high frequency
explanation: This is stated in the pitch definition.

fact: Ultrasound is sound above 20,000 Hz, beyond human hearing.
explanation: The glossary defines ultrasound this way.

fact: Infrasound is sound below a frequency of 20 Hz.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of infrasound.

fact: The auditory range is the lowest and highest frequencies a type of animal can hear.
acceptedAnswers: lowest and highest frequencies an animal can hear
explanation: This is the glossary definition of auditory range.
commonMistakes: Thinking every animal has the same range

fact: The pinna is the outside part of the ear that we can see.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of pinna.
commonMistakes: Calling the cochlea the outside ear

fact: The auditory canal is the passage in the ear from the outer ear to the eardrum.
acceptedAnswers: passage from outer ear to eardrum
explanation: This is the glossary definition of auditory canal.
commonMistakes: Confusing auditory canal with auditory nerve

fact: The eardrum is a membrane that transmits sound vibrations from the outer ear to the middle ear.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of eardrum.

fact: The ossicles are small bones that transfer vibrations from the eardrum to the oval window.
acceptedAnswers: transfer vibrations from eardrum to oval window
explanation: This is the glossary definition of ossicle.
commonMistakes: Saying ossicles are in the eye

fact: The cochlea is a snail-shaped tube in the inner ear with sensory cells that detect sound.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of cochlea.

fact: An electrical signal travels along the auditory nerve to the brain.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of auditory nerve.
commonMistakes: Saying sound waves travel directly into the brain

fact: The speed of light is 300 million m/s.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of speed of light.

fact: A luminous object gives out light.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of luminous.
commonMistakes: Saying the Moon is luminous because it reflects light

fact: A non-luminous object produces no light.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of non-luminous.
commonMistakes: Saying non-luminous objects give out light

fact: An opaque material allows no light to pass through it.
acceptedAnswers: allows no light to pass through
explanation: This is the glossary definition of opaque.

fact: A translucent material allows some light to pass through it.
acceptedAnswers: allows some light to pass through
explanation: This is the glossary definition of translucent.
commonMistakes: Saying translucent allows all light through

fact: A transparent material allows all light to pass through it.
acceptedAnswers: allows all light to pass through
explanation: This is the glossary definition of transparent.

fact: Reflection is a change in direction of light or sound when it hits a boundary and bounces back.
acceptedAnswers: change in direction when it hits a boundary and bounces back
explanation: This is the glossary definition of reflection.
commonMistakes: Saying reflection is bending into a new material

fact: The law of reflection says the angle of incidence is equal to the angle of reflection.
acceptedAnswers: angle of incidence equals angle of reflection
explanation: This is the glossary definition of law of reflection.
commonMistakes: Saying the reflected angle is always zero

fact: The normal line is an imaginary line at right angles to the surface, used to measure angles.
acceptedAnswers: imaginary line at right angles to surface
explanation: This is the glossary definition of normal line.
commonMistakes: Measuring angles from the mirror surface

fact: Specular reflection is reflection from a smooth surface.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of specular reflection.
commonMistakes: Saying rough surfaces make specular reflection

fact: Diffuse reflection is reflection from a rough surface.
explanation: This is the glossary definition of diffuse reflection.
commonMistakes: Saying diffuse reflection makes a clear mirror image

fact: Refraction is a change in direction of light going from one material into another.
acceptedAnswers: change in direction going from one material into another
explanation: This is the glossary definition of refraction.

fact: A convex lens is thicker in the middle and bends light rays towards each other.
acceptedAnswers: thicker in the middle and bends rays towards each other
explanation: This is the glossary definition of convex.

fact: A concave lens is thinner in the middle and spreads out light rays.
acceptedAnswers: thinner in the middle and spreads out light rays
explanation: This is the glossary definition of concave.

fact: The primary colours of light are red, blue and green.
